refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, excluding planned maintenance windows.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement period and baseline, (2) what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable' (e.g., excludes planned maintenance, includes degraded modes), (3) the evaluation methodology, and (4) the consequence of non-compliance. High safety criticality demands that availability targets not compromise safety protocols—availability must be achieved through reliable design, not by deferring safety checks.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs) from deployed NGHE units over the agreed evaluation period (e.g., 12 months). Calculate availability as (Total Scheduled Time − Unplanned Downtime) / Total Scheduled Time. Validate against customer-defined target. Conduct root-cause analysis on any downtime events to identify systemic failure modes.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime by detecting and isolating faults before they cause mission loss.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall provide predictive maintenance capabilities to schedule maintenance during planned windows and reduce surprise failures.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall enable rapid fault recovery and system restart to minimize downtime duration when failures occur.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain safety-critical functions (e.g., emergency stop, collision avoidance) even during partial system degradation to prevent safety-driven unavailability.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant hardware and software subsystems with automatic failover to mask single-point failures and maintain continuous operation.
DP-B: Deploy real-time health monitoring and predictive diagnostics across all subsystems to detect degradation early and trigger preventive maintenance before failure.
DP-C: Design modular, hot-swappable components with standardized interfaces to enable rapid field replacement and minimize repair time.
DP-D: Establish a tiered availability strategy combining redundancy (for critical paths), health monitoring (for early warning), and rapid recovery (for non-critical paths).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Establish a tiered availability strategy combining redundancy (for critical safety-critical and mission-critical paths), health monitoring and predictive diagnostics (for early fault detection and planned maintenance), and rapid recovery procedures (for non-critical subsystems). This strategy balances cost, complexity, and safety by applying the appropriate availability mechanism to each functional tier.

potential_sources: Redundancy design must not introduce new failure modes (e.g., common-cause failures in redundant channels).
Health monitoring requires real-time data collection and analysis; coupling with telemetry and network infrastructure is expected and manageable.
Rapid recovery procedures depend on modular design and standardized interfaces; this couples to vehicle architecture and component design.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a composite strategy that decouples the availability problem into three orthogonal mechanisms: (1) redundancy for critical functions, (2) monitoring for early warning, (3) recovery for resilience. This reduces coupling compared to single-mechanism approaches (e.g., DP-A alone would over-specify redundancy for non-critical functions). The strategy is compatible with high safety criticality because it preserves safety-critical functions even during partial degradation.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer accepts the cost and complexity trade-offs of a tiered strategy (redundancy is expensive; monitoring adds overhead).
Assumption: Predictive diagnostics algorithms can be validated to sufficient confidence for safety-critical decisions.
Risk: Redundancy design may introduce latent common-cause failures (e.g., both channels fail due to environmental stress) that are difficult to detect in testing.
Risk: Health monitoring data quality and algorithm accuracy directly impact availability; poor diagnostics will trigger false alarms or miss real faults.
Risk: Rapid recovery procedures require operator training and may not be feasible in all field conditions (e.g., remote worksites with limited support).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) which subsystems receive redundancy (e.g., propulsion, steering, safety sensors) vs. monitoring-only (e.g., comfort features), (2) the health monitoring architecture (centralized vs. distributed), (3) the recovery time objective (RTO) and recovery point objective (RPO) for each tier, and (4) the validation approach for predictive diagnostics (e.g., field trials, statistical confidence intervals).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. At this level, the IM assignment for availability (DP-D) resides in the NGHE Solution as the orchestrating entity that coordinates tiered availability strategies across all subordinate modules. The NGHE Solution owns the availability KPI and is responsible for defining, implementing, and verifying the tiered strategy.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Health monitoring and diagnostic data flows from Vehicle and Safety Services to external monitoring systems (e.g., fleet management, predictive maintenance platforms). Telemetry includes uptime/downtime logs, fault events, and system state snapshots.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Mission tasking and operational directives from Site Manager or Tele-operator. Availability strategy must ensure tasking can be received and processed even during partial system degradation (e.g., reduced-capability modes).

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts and emergency notifications from Safety Services. Availability strategy must guarantee SafetyAlert delivery even if other subsystems are degraded or recovering.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication backbone for teleoperation, health monitoring, and remote diagnostics. Network Link availability directly impacts the effectiveness of health monitoring and remote recovery procedures.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the availability KPI and tiered strategy. It orchestrates redundancy, monitoring, and recovery across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Minimal diffusion: the strategy is defined at the system level and delegated to subordinate modules for implementation. High containment because availability is a system-level property that emerges from the integration of all modules.

Vehicle implements redundancy and health monitoring for propulsion, steering, and mobility subsystems. However, Vehicle alone cannot achieve the availability target because it does not control Teleoperation or Safety Services. Significant diffusion: availability strategy is split across Vehicle (hardware redundancy, local diagnostics) and NGHE Solution (orchestration, tiered strategy).

Teleoperation contributes to availability by enabling remote operation and recovery when local systems are degraded. However, Teleoperation is not the primary owner of availability; it is a supporting capability. High diffusion: availability depends on Vehicle, Safety Services, and Network Link, which are outside Teleoperation's scope.

Safety Services implements health monitoring and fault detection for safety-critical functions. It contributes to availability by enabling early fault detection and preventing safety-driven unavailability. However, Safety Services does not own the overall tiered strategy or redundancy design. Moderate diffusion: availability strategy is split between Safety Services (monitoring) and Vehicle (redundancy).

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of the availability KPI and tiered strategy because: (1) availability is a system-level emergent property that depends on the integration of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, (2) the tiered strategy requires orchestration across multiple modules (e.g., deciding when to activate redundancy, when to trigger remote recovery), and (3) the NGHE Solution is responsible for defining and verifying the overall KPI. Subordinate modules (Vehicle, Safety Services) implement specific mechanisms (redundancy, monitoring) but do not own the strategy.
containment_risks: Risk: If the tiered strategy is not explicitly documented and owned at the NGHE Solution level, responsibility for availability may diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, leading to gaps or conflicts.

Risk: The NGHE Solution module may lack the detailed technical authority to enforce redundancy and monitoring requirements on Vehicle and Safety Services if those modules are owned by different teams or suppliers.

Risk: Availability measurement and verification require integration of telemetry from all modules; if telemetry collection is not coordinated at the NGHE Solution level, data quality and completeness may suffer.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit ownership and governance: (1) NGHE Solution owns the availability KPI, tiered strategy definition, and end-to-end verification. (2) Vehicle owns the implementation of redundancy and local health monitoring for propulsion and mobility subsystems. (3) Safety Services owns the implementation of health monitoring and fault detection for safety-critical functions. (4) Teleoperation owns the implementation of remote recovery capabilities. (5) Define clear interfaces and handoff points between modules (e.g., Vehicle reports health status to NGHE Solution via Telemetry; NGHE Solution decides whether to activate remote recovery via Teleoperation). (6) Establish a cross-module availability review board to coordinate strategy and resolve conflicts.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability over the evaluation period) is clearly mapped to DP-D (tiered availability strategy combining redundancy, monitoring, and recovery). The DP directly addresses the FR by specifying how to minimize unplanned downtime and maintain continuous operation. The DP is feasible and aligns with industry best practices for high-availability systems. No decoupling or coupling issues identified at this level.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: The tiered strategy is defined at the NGHE Solution level but implemented across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. If implementation responsibilities are not clearly assigned, the strategy may not be fully realized.
Diffusion Risk 2: Health monitoring requires real-time data collection from Vehicle and Safety Services; if telemetry infrastructure is not robust, monitoring effectiveness will degrade.
Diffusion Risk 3: Rapid recovery procedures depend on Teleoperation and Vehicle capabilities; if these modules do not implement the required recovery mechanisms, the strategy will fail.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of the availability strategy, but implementation is distributed across subordinate modules. This is acceptable if clear interfaces and responsibilities are defined. The Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link interfaces are well-defined and support the tiered strategy. Containment is strong at the strategy level (NGHE Solution) but requires careful coordination at the implementation level (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability can be verified by collecting operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs) from deployed NGHE units and calculating the availability ratio. This is a standard KPI measurement approach. Verification requires: (1) robust telemetry collection from Vehicle and Safety Services, (2) clear definition of what constitutes 'available' vs. 'unavailable', (3) agreed evaluation period and baseline, and (4) root-cause analysis on downtime events. All of these are feasible with proper instrumentation and governance.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The availability KPI is allocated to the NGHE Solution module, which is responsible for defining and verifying the tiered strategy. Subordinate modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are responsible for implementing specific mechanisms (redundancy, monitoring, recovery) that contribute to the overall availability target. Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Explicitly define the availability measurement methodology in the refined FR (e.g., calculation formula, exclusions for planned maintenance, confidence intervals).
Revision 2: Establish a cross-module availability review board to coordinate strategy implementation and resolve conflicts between Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

Revision 3: Define clear success criteria for each tier of the strategy (e.g., redundancy shall mask single-point failures with <1 second switchover time; monitoring shall detect faults with >95% accuracy; recovery shall restore operation within <5 minutes).
Revision 4: Specify the validation approach for predictive diagnostics algorithms (e.g., field trials, statistical confidence intervals, comparison to ground truth).

decision.justification: The refined FR (CR-NGHE-004) is a high-level KPI that requires decomposition into lower-level functional requirements to be implementable. The selected DP (tiered availability strategy) is sound and well-mapped to the FR, but it is a composite strategy that encompasses multiple mechanisms (redundancy, monitoring, recovery). To proceed with detailed design and implementation, each mechanism must be decomposed into specific functional requirements and design parameters. The sub-FRs identified (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.4) are appropriate starting points for the next decomposition level. The NGHE Solution module is the correct owner at this level, but subordinate modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) will own specific sub-FRs in the next level.
decision.justification_detail: CR-NGHE-004 is a KPI-level requirement that cannot be directly implemented without further decomposition. The tiered strategy (DP-D) is a sound approach, but it requires specification of: (1) which subsystems receive redundancy, (2) the health monitoring architecture, (3) the recovery time objectives, and (4) the validation approach. These details will emerge from the decomposition of sub-FRs. Additionally, the high safety criticality of the system demands that availability be achieved without compromising safety; this constraint must be explicitly addressed in the sub-FRs (e.g., FR-NGHE-004.4).
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime by detecting and isolating faults before they cause mission loss. [Owned by Safety Services and Vehicle]
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall provide predictive maintenance capabilities to schedule maintenance during planned windows and reduce surprise failures. [Owned by Safety Services and Vehicle]
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall enable rapid fault recovery and system restart to minimize downtime duration when failures occur. [Owned by Teleoperation and Vehicle]

FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain safety-critical functions (e.g., emergency stop, collision avoidance) even during partial system degradation to prevent safety-driven unavailability. [Owned by Safety Services]

assumptions: Assumption 1: Customer accepts the cost and complexity trade-offs of a tiered availability strategy (redundancy is expensive; monitoring adds overhead).
Assumption 2: Predictive diagnostics algorithms can be validated to sufficient confidence for safety-critical decisions.
Assumption 3: The evaluation period for availability measurement is at least 12 months to capture seasonal variations and long-term reliability trends.
Assumption 4: Planned maintenance windows are excluded from the availability calculation; only unplanned downtime counts against the target.
Assumption 5: The NGHE Solution module has sufficient technical authority to enforce availability requirements on subordinate modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).
Assumption 6: Network Link is sufficiently reliable to support real-time health monitoring and remote recovery procedures.
open_risks: Risk 1: Redundancy design may introduce latent common-cause failures (e.g., both channels fail due to environmental stress) that are difficult to detect in testing.
Risk 2: Health monitoring data quality and algorithm accuracy directly impact availability; poor diagnostics will trigger false alarms or miss real faults.
Risk 3: Rapid recovery procedures require operator training and may not be feasible in all field conditions (e.g., remote worksites with limited support).
Risk 4: If the tiered strategy is not explicitly documented and owned at the NGHE Solution level, responsibility for availability may diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
Risk 5: Availability measurement requires integration of telemetry from all modules; if telemetry collection is not coordinated at the NGHE Solution level, data quality and completeness may suffer.
Risk 6: High safety criticality may conflict with availability targets if safety checks are deferred to improve uptime; this tension must be explicitly managed.
evidence_needed: Evidence 1: Field trial data from similar high-availability systems demonstrating achievability of ≥0.97 availability with tiered strategy.
Evidence 2: Validation results for predictive diagnostics algorithms (e.g., accuracy, false positive rate, detection latency).
Evidence 3: Redundancy design analysis showing common-cause failure modes and mitigation strategies.
Evidence 4: Telemetry architecture design and data quality specifications.
Evidence 5: Rapid recovery procedure documentation and operator training materials.
Evidence 6: Cross-module interface specifications and handoff procedures.
Evidence 7: Safety analysis (FMEA, FTA) demonstrating that availability strategy does not compromise safety-critical functions.
